Title: One man, his dog, and ChatGPT: Australia's AI vaccine saga
Article:
HONG KONG: Desperate to help his sick dog, one Australian man went down the ultimate ChatGPT research hole, using artificial intelligence to design a personalised experimental treatment and finding top scientists to administer it.
Paul Conyngham's months-long quest to fight his rescue mutt Rosie's cancer has grabbed the attention of OpenAI boss Sam Altman, who called it an "amazing story" in an X post on Friday.
Sydney-based AI consultant Conyngham told AFP that eight-year-old Rosie's mast cell cancer is now in partial remission and her biggest tumour has shrunk dramatically.
"She regained a lot of mobility and function" after receiving a custom mRNA vaccine along with powerful immunotherapy in December, he said.
Conyngham does not call his findings a cure – but experts unrelated to the dogged endeavour said they highlight AI's potential to accelerate medical research.
"I would have conversations and just keep them going non-stop" with ChatGPT, Gemini and Grok to study cancer therapies in-depth, Conyngham said.
Following the chatbots' advice, he paid $3,000 to have Rosie's genome sequenced, and used the same online tools to analyse her DNA data.
Next, he turned to AlphaFold, a scientific AI model that won 2024's chemistry Nobel, to better understand one of the mutated doggy genes.
Conyngham sought the help of a University of New South Wales (UNSW) team – also thanks to a ChatGPT recommendation – and other academics in Australia who made his research a reality.
'JUST A RASH'
Rosie's cancer was misdiagnosed for nearly a year, Conyngham said on the phone during one of the long daily walks the pair have resumed.
"I took her to the vet three times. And two times, the vet said, don't worry about it, it's just a rash," he said.
But Rosie got sicker, and a biopsy showed in 2024 that she did have terminal cancer.
Having tried chemotherapy, standard immunotherapy and surgery, costs were mounting, and Conyngham wanted more options.
So he used AI to delve deep into the world of emerging treatments including mRNA vaccines, which train the body's immune system and were widely used during the Covid pandemic.
"This was not a clinical trial by any means" and "it's not that AI cured cancer", said UNSW professor Martin Smith, who sequenced Rosie's genome for Paul.
"It was really driven by his determination to help his dog."
The combination of "three different disruptive technologies: genome sequencing, artificial intelligence, and RNA therapeutics… offers new possibilities and challenges", Smith said.
AI PROMISE
Chatbots also assisted Conyngham in navigating the reams of paperwork for ethical approval.
And through his new scientific network, he met a professor at the University of Queensland able to administer the fine-tuned treatment.
Not all the tumours responded as well as the largest one, however. Rosie has had to have another operation since, and it's unclear how long she has left to live.
"The short answer is we don't know for sure" what actually led to the reduction in size of Rosie's biggest tumour, said Pall Thordarson, director of UNSW's RNA institute which created the vaccine.
"He used the AI program… to design the actual mRNA sequence. And then he gave that information to us," Thordarson explained.
"AI holds lots of promise to improve and accelerate our research strategies," Nick Semenkovich at the Medical College of Wisconsin, unrelated to the Rosie saga, told AFP.
But UNSW and Conyngham "haven't published scientific details outside of their press release and interviews, so we don't know enough about the vaccine to understand how much AI helped in its development – or if the vaccine worked the way it was designed", Semenkovich said.
Patrick Tang Ming-kuen, a professor from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, said AI-powered research could help pets and humans survive, although the risk of errors is real.
"AI transforms a 'needle-in-a-haystack' search into a data-driven selection process, drastically shortening the timeframe between diagnosis and vaccine construction," he said.
Since Conyngham's story went global, Smith said his team have been fielding various new requests.
"You know: my cat's got a disease, my dog's got a disease, my aunt has got a disease."
But "it's hard for us to be able to help", he said. "There's a lot of things that have to align."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMEA rescues 13 tourists after boat stalls at sea
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: The Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) rescued 13 tourists after their boat stalled off Kuala Besut yesterday afternoon.
State MMEA director Captain Hamiludin Che Awang said the group, which included 12 foreigners, was rescued by an MMEA team on a routine patrol.
He said the patrol team spotted the boat, approximately 7.3 nautical miles from the Kuala Besut estuary, drifting, and passengers waving for help.
The boat had suffered engine damage.
Hamiludin said all 13 passengers - four from China, three from France, two each from the United Kingdom and Singapore, and one each from Bangladesh and Malaysia - were brought to the Besut MMEA jetty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KJ's suit against Jamal to be heard in Sessions Court
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The High Court here today ruled that former Health Minister Khairy Jamaluddin's application to file a lawsuit against Sungai Besar Umno division chief Jamal Yunos must be heard at the Sessions Court.
Judge Datuk Anand Ponnudurai made the decision on the grounds that claims filed in the High Court must involve amounts exceeding RM1 million.
Lawyer V. Mugunthan, representing Jamal as the defendant, said his client was prepared to issue an apology, although the plaintiff was also seeking damages.
"The judge ordered that the suit be transferred to the Sessions Court, as it is unlikely the plaintiff would be awarded damages exceeding RM1 million," he told reporters after the proceedings.
Earlier, lawyer Sheetal Kaur said the plaintiff was prepared to accept any amount proposed by the defendant, although no figure had been agreed upon.
"We are ready to accept whatever amount is offered, but at this stage, no sum has been determined. The defendant is only willing to apologise, whereas the case has drawn attention as the plaintiff is a former minister and politician," she said.
On Sept 19, 2023, Jamal filed an application to strike out the suit, citing, among other grounds, that the claim against him was unclear, embarrassing and an abuse of the court process.
Khairy, popularly known as KJ, filed the suit on May 24, 2023, saying that during the 2022 Umno General Assembly on Jan 14, 2023, Jamal held a press conference at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur attended by media practitioners, Umno delegates, as well as party members and leaders.
The plaintiff alleged that on the same day, Jamal uploaded a video titled "I hope Khairy Jamaluddin will be subjected to action by the Umno Disciplinary Board after making reckless statements that embarrass Umno #UMNODuluKiniDanSelamanya" on Facebook under the account "DS Jamal Yunos (DS Jamal Yunos Official)".
The former Umno Youth chief said the defendant also uploaded the same recording on a TikTok account under the name "jamal_yunos".
Khairy further alleged that the defamatory statements implied, among other things, that he was a leader without integrity, unfit to lead, and disloyal to Umno and its leadership.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: World Bank Group's IFC, OCI TerraSus team up to advance Malaysia's semiconductor push
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The World Bank Group, via its private-sector arm the International Finance Corporation (IFC), has partnered with OCI TerraSus Sdn Bhd (OCI TRS), a unit of South Korea's OCI Holdings Company Ltd, to advance Malaysia's participation in the global semiconductor supply chain and create jobs in the country.
They said the partnership would also drive enhancements in IFC's environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards and management systems.
"Central to this collaboration is the development of a clean energy-powered manufacturing facility for ultra-high purity semiconductor-grade polysilicon in Bintulu, Sarawak, through a joint venture with Japan's Tokuyama Corporation.
"Through this partnership with IFC and expansion in the global semiconductor supply chain, OCITRS targets active enhancements to its corporate governance and management systems, ESGcapabilities, technical advancements, competitiveness and long-term resilience," they said in a joint statement.
They said that under this collaboration, IFC will provide OCI TRS with a loan facility of up to US$125 million (US$1=RM4.01), which will primarily be used to construct the state-of-the-art manufacturing facility, the first of its kind in Southeast Asia.
OCI Holdings and OCI TerraSus chairman Lee Woo Hyun said the partnership is a key driver in strengthening management systems, ESG practices and long-term competitiveness as a global company.
"As demand grows for semiconductors and artificial intelligence, the importance of high-purity materials will continue to increase," he said.
World Bank Group country manager for Malaysia Judith Green said Malaysia has significant ambitions to move up the value chain in value-added manufacturing industries, which are major job creators, making them a key area of focus for the group.
"We are delighted to support the country towards fulfilling these ambitions. We are also encouraged by OCI TerraSus' commitment to strengthening its ESG standards, and look forward to supporting the company in achieving its commitments," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Philippine peso hits record low, Indonesian rupiah steadies after support steps
Article:
BENGALURU: The Philippine peso hit a record low on Monday and the Indonesian rupiah stopped short of touching a historic trough of 17,000 per dollar as the country's central bank introduced a new tool for banks to manage foreign-currency liquidity.
The Philippine peso hit a low of 60.776 per dollar, while stocks in Manila tumbled 2.5 per cent, as the net oil importergrapples with an "imminent danger of a critically low energy supply."
A number of oil-importing countries in the region have been hit hard by the US-Israeli war on Iran, which has effectively closed off the Strait of Hormuz, a key transit point for Middle Eastern oil.
Indonesia, another oil-importing country, continues to experience massive outflows due to concerns about the impact of elevated oil prices, compounding persistent concerns over fiscal and governance risks.
Jakarta stocks fell more than 2 per cent, extending monthly losses to around 14 per cent and putting them on course for their worst month since March 2020.
Foreign investors have pulled out 21.37 trillion rupiah (US$1.26 billion) from Indonesia's stock markets so far this month, the largest outflow in at least over a decade, according to LSEG data.
Its currency, the rupiah, wallowed at around 16,900 a dollar, a stone's throw from its record low of 17,000. Bank Indonesia on Monday implemented new foreign exchange repo transactions using its FX-denominated securities, providing banks with an alternative for liquidity management, particularly foreign exchange liquidity.
"The instruments function as high-quality, short-term collateral: Banks obtain the dollars they need, repay the loan when cash returns, while the collateral stays on the balance sheet," said Wei Li, head of multi-asset investments at BNP Paribas.
"The repo's market-neutral design limits immediate pressure on the IDR/USD rate, unlike a direct spot sale that would inject rupiah liquidity." Elsewhere in emerging markets, the Indian rupee rallied sharply to 93.85 per dollar after the central bank tightened limits on banks' foreign exchange positions.
Malaysia's benchmark stock index dipped 1.5 per cent, while stocks in South Korea and Taiwan fell as much as 5.3 per cent and 2.4 per cent. The conflict in the Middle East and its impact on global energy prices and supply continues to roil financial markets worldwide.
Oil-importing Asian emerging economies are particularly exposed, with higher prices already triggering capital outflows and pressuring currencies.
Foreign investors have pulled 34.71 billion baht (US$1.06 billion) out of Thai equities, while stock markets in South Korea and Taiwan have seen outflows worth US$19.71 billion and US$25.74 billion, respectively, exchange and LSEG data showed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Caretaker jailed over neglect linked to Baby Syifaa's death
Article:
SEREMBAN: A caretaker was sentenced to one year's imprisonment by the Sessions Court here today for neglect that led to the death of 15-month-old Nour Rania Asyifaa Yuseri, known as Baby Syifaa, four years ago.
Judge N Kanageswari handed down the sentence to Khairunnisa Ahmad Damanhuri, 41, at the conclusion of proceedings involving the incident at a childcare centre in Bandar Sri Sendayan.
The court also imposed a RM15,000 fine, with an additional four months' imprisonment in default. Khairunnisa must complete 48 hours of community service within six months after serving her jail term, and is subject to a one-year good behaviour bond of RM5,000, to be secured by a family member.
Khairunnisa was represented by lawyer S Kavittha, while the prosecution was led by Negeri Sembilan State Public Prosecutor Ku Hayati Ku Haron. Baby Syifaa's parents, Anida Idrus, 44, and Yuseri Yusoff, 42, were present in court.
According to the charge, Khairunnisa, as the person entrusted with the child's care, had neglected her in a manner likely to cause physical injury. The offence was alleged to have occurred about 12.30pm on Feb 22, 2022.
She was charged under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001, which provides for a maximum penalty of 20 years' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM50,000, or both.
On July 31, 2024, the Coroner's Court ruled that the child's death was caused by neck compression after she was found unconscious in her cot at the centre.
A total of 28 witnesses, including the child's parents, testified during the inquest, which began on Sept 22, 2022.
Yuseri had told the court that his daughter's death was not caused by her head being caught in the cot's fabric, but by strangulation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hot weather boosts Harumanis quality, sweetness
Article:
ALOR STAR: Prolonged hot weather in the northern peninsula has proven to be a 'bonus' for mango farmers, with this year's Harumanis harvest reported to be sweeter and of higher quality.
USIMA Agriculture company director Ahmad Ikhram Mohd Yusof said high temperatures and dry conditions play a crucial role in the fruit-ripening process, naturally increasing the sugar content of Harumanis.
"When the weather is hot and there is less rain, the fruit becomes sweeter, its flesh denser and its aroma stronger.
"That is the difference between Harumanis D'Aman and those from the normal season," he said during a visit to his plantation in Tok Keling, Alor Mengkudu, today.
Ahmad Ikhram, who also manages the Harumanis plantation, said this year's weather is ideal for producing premium fruit, with quality more consistent than in previous years.
He said last year the plantation produced 17,000 Harumanis fruits, while this year the yield has increased to 30,000.
"The hot weather has also reduced fungal attacks, resulting in fruit that is of better quality and requiring less frequent pesticide use.
"As in previous years, we sell Harumanis according to different grades and qualities, but this time we are offering a uniform grade and quality at RM40 per kilo," he said.
Meanwhile, State Agriculture, Plantation and Transportation Committee chairman Dzowahir Ab Ghani said hot weather is a key factor in determining the sweetness of Harumanis.
He added that Kedah's Harumanis is renowned for its sweetness, and favourable weather conditions are important in maintaining its premium quality.
"To date, it is estimated that there are approximately 150,000 Harumanis trees planted in Kedah, with a density of around 150 trees per hectare.
"In the next five years, this number is expected to increase to 500,000 trees to meet rising market demand.
"The public can get more information about the crop as well as the different government assistance at the Mempelam Harum Manis Festival, which will be held for three days starting April 30," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taiping Lake Gardens water levels dip slightly, paddle boating continues
Article:
TAIPING: Water levels at Taiping Lake Gardens remain largely stable, with only a slight decline observed.
Taiping Municipal Council president Mohamed Akmal Dahalan said that recent rainfall had helped sustain the lake's level despite ongoing dry weather.
"Dry conditions are unpredictable, but we are beginning to see a pattern emerging. We hope such a situation will not occur in the near future," he told reporters after attending the launch of the refurbished interior of the Taiping Clock Tower today.
He said that while there had been some reduction, it was not yet significant.
"So far, the drop is hardly noticeable. There has been a slight decrease, but yesterday's rain helped," he added.
Akmal said that recreational activities, including paddle boating, continued to operate as usual.
"Boats can still be used. If the water level drops further and the lake dries up, operations would be affected," he said.
However, he cautioned that a prolonged decline in water levels could affect the visual appeal of the popular park.
"When the water recedes, the area becomes less attractive as the lakebed is exposed. We hope this does not happen, or if it does, it will only be temporary," he added.
Akmal said the council would continue to monitor the situation closely and expressed hope that weather conditions remained favourable in the coming weeks.
Taiping Lake Gardens, the first public recreational garden established in the country, is home to rain trees that are over 100 years old. Known locally as hujan-hujan trees, the monkey pod tree bears the scientific name Samanea saman.
In 2024, despite being recognised as the wettest town in Peninsular Malaysia, Taiping experienced a drought, which led to some sections of the lake drying up due to extreme weather conditions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Drought leaves Kelantan padi farmers facing RM1mil in losses
Article:
KOTA BARU: More than 10 padi farmers here have suffered losses amounting to RM1 million after their crops failed due to an ongoing dry spell.
The affected farmers are from several villages, including Kampung Belukar, Kampung Salor and Kampung Beta Hilir.
Farmers' representative Hafizul Daud from the Kemubu Agricultural Development Authority (Kada) Tanjung Puri said about 50 hectares of padi fields had been affected in what he described as the worst drought in six planting seasons.
"We hope Kada and the relevant departments will come to inspect the situation on the ground.
"To save the crops, we have to use water pumps, but the cost is very high. We hope the government can assist us as much as possible," he said when met today.
Hafizul added that farmers were also facing difficulties due to issues with water supply from the Kemubu irrigation system, forcing them to incur additional costs to draw water.
"This is the sixth season and the worst so far. We have many commitments, including schooling for our children.
"We have already spent heavily on machinery and other costs, and now we are facing losses," he said.
Another farmer, Mohd Izat Afiq Mohd Yusof, 32, said he had suffered losses of about RM10,000 per season after his eight hectares of padi fields in Salor failed due to insufficient water supply.
"I was hoping for a full harvest, but the situation has been exhausting. For the past five seasons, water has not reached our fields.
"I had to use my own water pumps, but fuel costs alone reach about RM70 a day for one pump. If we use three to five pumps, the cost becomes too high.
"Our area does have water sources through tube wells, but during prolonged drought, the supply is still not enough," he said.
He urged the authorities, including Kada, to better monitor the irrigation system and provide assistance to affected farmers.
Another farmer, Sidik Ahmad Mohamad Nor, 65, said all four hectares of his padi fields had dried up.
"I have been farming since I was 20, and this is among the toughest situations I have faced.
"The land has completely dried up. We are exhausted, and fuel prices have also increased, adding to our burden.
"Previously, there was water supply around October, but it did not last long before it stopped again," he said.
Sidik, who has five children, called on the Agriculture and Food Security Ministry to step in and provide immediate assistance.
"We hope the Agriculture Minister will look into our plight and extend aid to ease our burden," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unregulated houses of worship: 70pct of online comments AI-generated, says deputy minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: About 70 per cent of online comments surrounding the recent unregulated houses of worship controversy were AI-generated, Deputy National Unity Minister Yuneswaran Ramaraj said, warning that the issue was being weaponised for political ends.
Yuneswaran said the ministry had conducted research on the online discourse surrounding the temple issue and found the engagement patterns to be suspicious.
"Imagine — after you post something, within one hour you have 10,000 likes and 800 comments. And this was on a Monday morning at 11am.
"On a Monday, everyone is at work. But the engagement that morning was abnormally high," he said, adding that repetitive comments were another tell-tale sign of coordinated activity.
"We can detect comments that I believe are AI-generated, fake accounts, with malicious intent to stir up racial and religious issues. They are using this as a political weapon," he said.
He was speaking to reporters at the launch of Pusat Komas' Malaysia Racism Report 2025 at Sultan Abdul Samad Building today.
Yuneswaran said that at its core, the temple dispute was a civil matter between a house of worship and a landowner, not a racial or religious one.
"It is a civil case. It has nothing to do with religious affairs. When it gets into the politicians' hands, then it becomes a religious issue," he said, adding that swift action by local authorities, state governments and elected representatives could have prevented it from escalating.
"When they cannot resolve it, it goes to the 'heroes of the nation' — politicians — who create more chaos. And this is real," he said.
He called on all parties to stay away from race, religion and royalty (3R) issues, saying such matters transcended politics and threatened the social harmony built over generations.
"We cannot ride on 3R matters. It will tear the fabric of society that we have built over many years. That should be the central point," he said.
On Feb 9, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the government
would not permit
the construction of unregulated houses of worship and that local authorities are authorised to 'clear' those built without proper legal approval.
The prime minister clarified that the policy is not new, but a continuation of the government's stance across multiple administrations.
Malaysia Hindu Sangam (MHS) president T. Ganesan has since expressed support for
Anwar's call
, adding that issues surrounding Hindu temples are long-standing, rooted in the country's history, heritage, and socio-economic development both before and after independence. ⁠)
Ganesan said the existence of temples without formal approval often results from historical constraints, previous governance weaknesses, changes in land status, and unresolved early-stage issues, rather than solely from non-compliance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreign funds return to Bursa Malaysia, buck regional outflows
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Foreign investors turned net buyers last week, snapping a two-week streak of net selling with RM587.6 million in net inflows, according to MBSB Investment Bank Bhd.
Last week marked the sixth consecutive week of net foreign outflows across Asian markets, totalling US$15.68 billion, the bank said in its fund flow report for the week ended March 27.
Among eight Asian markets tracked, only Malaysia and Thailand recorded net foreign inflows, while outflows were led by South Korea, India, Taiwan, Indonesia and the Philippines.
On Bursa Malaysia, foreign investors were net buyers on all four trading days, with the largest inflow recorded on Wednesday at RM191.5 million, followed by Friday (RM176.2 million) and Thursday (RM112.9 million).
"The top three sectors that recorded net foreign inflows were plantation (RM385.9 million), financial services (RM295.6 million) and energy (RM139.1 million)," the bank said.
Meanwhile, the sectors that saw the largest net foreign outflows were technology (RM123.1 million), consumer products and services (RM89.2 million) and construction (RM78.5 million).
Local institutions turned net sellers, ending a two-week streak of net buying with outflows totalling RM582.9 million.
Retail investors also extended their selling streak to two weeks, recording net outflows of RM4.7 million.
Average daily trading volume declined 0.4 per cent among retail investors and 14.7 per cent among foreign investors, but rose 1.1 per cent among local institutions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tony Fernandes: Demand defies fuel spikes, still better than Covid-19 grounding
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Low-cost carrier AirAsia is still seeing strong flight demand despite rising fuel prices that are affecting aviation companies globally.
Capital A Bhd chief executive officer Tan Sri Tony Fernandes said the situation was "far worse" during Covid-19 as aircrafts were grounded.
"We couldn't fly during Covid but now we can fly. Demand is strong and in fact demand is better than before because more people are staying in this part of the world and not flying to the Europe and others," he said at Capital A's press conference today.
He said more details on the company's aviation arm will be discussed next week.
Fernandes said the strong demand enables the company to recover the extra costs as airline fares will have to go up.
"But our fares will go up much less than others and there are other opportunities that I will talk about next week," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stable consumption, subsidies to support consumer sector outlook
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The consumer products sector is expected to maintain earnings resilience and visibility, backed by stable domestic consumption and continued fiscal support.
RHB Research said measures such as Sumbangan Asas Rahmah and targeted fuel subsidies should help sustain consumer spending amid rising global energy prices.
"Under Budi95, while the monthly quota is reduced to 200 litres, 90 per cent of users consume below this level, implying limited immediate impact on the mass market.
"Overall, we do not expect any near-term policy shifts that could materially disrupt consumption trends. However, we remain watchful that a prolonged Middle East conflict could pose second-order risks via inflation," it said in a note.
The research house said nine out of 17 stocks under its coverage met earnings expectations for the fourth quarter of 2025, while seven disappointed and one exceeded expectations.
During the quarter, it downgraded Padini Holdings Bhd due to persistently weak sales momentum.
The sector saw a pick-up in consumer spending towards the year-end, particularly among discretionary players, driven by favourable seasonality.
This also suggests a steady consumption trend in line with improving consumer sentiment.
"Generally, management guidance was neutral to positive, with a number of discretionary players observing encouraging sales momentum since the start of 2026.
"This could be supported by firmer consumer sentiment, underpinned by solid macroeconomic conditions, healthy disposable income growth, a strengthening ringgit and positive spillover effects from Visit Malaysia Year 2026," it added.
RHB Research maintained its "Overweight" call on the sector, with Nestle (M) Bhd, Mr DIY Group (M) Bhd, Eco-Shop Marketing Bhd, Farm Fresh Bhd and Aeon Co (M) Bhd as its top picks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Online Safety Act: A vital shield against digital harm
Article:
LETTERS:
Malaysia's Online Safety Act (ONSA), which came into force on Jan 1 this year, is not only timely but essential.
ONSA represents a decisive and forward-looking response by the government to ensure that digital spaces remain safe, accountable, and responsibly managed.
It reflects a firm commitment to safeguarding public order, national security, and societal well-being in an increasingly complex information environment.
Importantly, ONSA addresses a wide spectrum of harmful content (as per the First Schedule), including:
* Child sexual abuse material, as provided under section 4 of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017;
* Financial fraud-related content; obscene and indecent content that offends standards of decency;
* Threatening, abusive, or insulting content causing harassment, fear, or distress;
* Content that may incite violence or terrorism;
* Content that encourages self-harm among children;
* Content that promotes hostility, ill-will, or disrupts public tranquillity; and,
* Content that promotes the use or sale of dangerous drugs.
Online harm does not exist in silos. It operates across emotional, psychological, and ideological dimensions, often with cumulative real-world consequences.
Among these categories, content that incites violence or supports terrorism remains particularly dangerous.
Such material can rapidly transition from digital influence to physical action, making early intervention not just desirable, but critical.
The urgency of this issue is illustrated by current global developments, particularly the ongoing tensions and conflict in the Middle East, including Iran.
Digital platforms have become saturated with highly emotive, polarising, and often unverified content related to these conflicts. Narratives are amplified in real time, some legitimate, others deeply misleading or deliberately inflammatory.
In the case of Iran, for instance, online spaces have been used not only to disseminate state and counter-state narratives, but also to mobilise ideological support, justify acts of violence, and deepen geopolitical divisions.
Crucially, ONSA recognises a fundamental reality of the digital age: harm is borderless. Its extraterritorial application ensures that the law applies not only within Malaysia but also to service providers operating outside the country, so long as their platforms are accessible to users in Malaysia.
By extending its scope beyond geographical boundaries, ONSA ensures that accountability is not limited by location but anchored in impact.
At the same time, it is crucial to emphasise that ONSA is not designed to suppress legitimate discourse. Freedom of speech remains a fundamental principle within Malaysia's legal framework.
However, like all rights, it is subject to reasonable and proportionate limits, particularly where national security, public order, and the protection of vulnerable groups are at stake.
ONSA targets content that crosses the threshold into harm, not ordinary disagreement, criticism, or debate.
With the appropriate safeguards in place, ONSA has the capacity to strike a careful and principled balance between protecting society and respecting individual rights.
Ultimately, ONSA recognises a simple but critical truth: what happens online does not stay online. It shapes perceptions, influences behaviour, and can have direct consequences for public safety and national stability.
By introducing ONSA, Malaysia is taking a firm, responsible, and necessary step in addressing the realities of the digital age.
It is about ensuring that digital spaces remain platforms for constructive engagement, not vehicles for harm.
DR HAEZREENA BEGUM ABDUL HAMID
Criminologist and Senior Lecturer
Faculty of Law, Universiti Malaya
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eight charged over assault of three policemen at PPR
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Eight men were charged in the magistrate's court with voluntarily causing hurt to three policemen in front of a flat last week.
Noor Nizar Shah Zainol Abidin, 37, Muhammad Syafiq Mohd Nordin, 28, Azwandy Azmi, 38, Muhamad Aiqal Zulkifrey, 28, Muhammad Firdaus Abdul Malik, 34, Muhammad Razin Abdul Muthalib, 34, Muhammad Syazwan Mohd Omar, 30, and Low Jia Hui, 26, claimed trial before magistrate Tunku Intan Nadiah Tunku Mohammed Nazarin.
They are alleged to have caused injuries to Lance Corporal S. Uvaneand, 26, Constable Muhammad Daniel Danish Azlan, 23, and Lance Corporal Muhammad Amzaruddin Fakhri Khuiruddin, 30, while the trio were carrying out their duties in front of the PPR flats at Jalan Sungai at 9.50pm on March 25.
The charge was framed under Section 332 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 149 of the same Code, which provides for up to three years' jail, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Siti Marissa Safina Saiful Adli requested bail at RM10,000 with one surety each.
Defence counsel S. Meharaj, in mitigation, pleaded for a minimal sum, noting that three of the accused had been in detention since March 25, while the others surrendered yesterday.
He said the men had cooperated with the police and reminded the court that bail was intended only to secure their attendance, not as a form of punishment.
The court heard that Noor Nizar Shah, a father of two, and Muhammad Syafiq run their own businesses, while Azwandy is a factory worker, and Muhamad Aiqal is a workshop clerk.
Muhammad Firdaus, who has high blood pressure, and Muhammad Razin, whose wife is heavily pregnant, are both fathers.
Muhammad Syazwan works as a runner, and Low is a chef.
Tunku Intan Nadiah set bail at RM5,000 each with one surety and fixed May 19 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Online learning won't affect students' progress, says Wong Kah Woh
Article:
TAIPING: The Education Ministry has assured that students' learning will not be disrupted if schools need to switch to online lessons due to the current hot weather.
Deputy Education Minister Wong Kah Woh said that while no cases of heat stroke among students had been reported so far, schools and authorities were prepared to implement Home-Based Teaching and Learning (PdPR) if necessary.
"This is not a new issue. Guidelines have been in place since 2023, and all school administrators, district education offices, and state education departments are familiar with the procedures. Decisions are made based on official weather reports and the needs of each school," he said.
Wong, who is also Taiping member of Parliament, told reporters after attending the swearing-in and appointment ceremony of Taiping Municipal Council members at the Taiping Municipal Building today.
He said that in two districts in Kedah, online learning had already been implemented as a precaution, and similar measures could be taken in other areas depending on local weather conditions.
"Online learning has been successfully implemented since the Covid-19 pandemic and during previous natural disasters, such as the storm that affected several schools in Selangor last year. It is not unfamiliar to us.
Even last year, when a storm struck Selangor, several schools conducted PdPR for over a month without issue. The most important thing when managing this situation is to prioritise the health and welfare of our children," he said.
Wong said that when PdPR was implemented, the ministry would ensure that learning continued effectively and efficiently.
"Whether it is PdPR or in-class learning, the schools are fully prepared," he said, adding that the ministry would continue monitoring the weather and make decisions based on the latest conditions.
It was reported that PdPR is currently being conducted in Kota Setar and Pendang, Kedah, from Sunday until tomorrow after
both districts
recorded Level 2 daily temperatures for three consecutive days.
A total of 86,030 students are involved, comprising 65,634 students from 154 schools in Kota Setar and 20,396 students from 52 schools in Pendang.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Racism cases in Malaysia hit record high in 2025
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Racism-related incidents in Malaysia hit a record high of 107 cases in 2025, marking the highest level in more than a decade, according to a new report.
"In 2025, we recorded 107 cases, the highest number in the eleven years since the start of this initiative," the report said.
The Malaysia Racism Report 2025 by Pusat Komas found that cases were largely driven by developments in public discourse and policy (45 cases), followed by Parliament (43 cases) and social settings (19 cases).
"Racism is increasingly shaped by public narratives and political rhetoric, rather than confined to isolated incidents," it said.
Incidents fluctuated throughout the year, peaking in August with 25 cases, while September recorded the lowest with just one case, suggesting incidents tend to cluster around parliamentary settings and key public debates.
Within Parliament, many cases stemmed from racial majoritarian narratives, xenophobic remarks and religion-based rhetoric, underscoring how race continues to feature prominently in national discourse.
"Parliament remains a key site where racial narratives are produced and amplified," it said.
The report said the high number of cases in Parliament is partly due to formal recording through Hansard, making such incidents more visible.
Beyond Parliament, prejudice and provocation emerged as the largest category with 17 cases, highlighting the persistence of interpersonal bias and discriminatory behaviour across society.
The report also cautioned that incidents in social settings may be underreported or less visible, while cases on social media — where much of today's racial discourse unfolds — remain largely unaccounted for.
"Online spaces, where much of today's discourse takes place, are not fully captured in current reporting," it said.
The findings echo longstanding concerns that racial discrimination remains a significant issue in Malaysia, cutting across sectors such as education, employment and public services.
"Racism is not only persistent, but increasingly normalised in public and institutional spaces," the report added.
The report suggests that racism in Malaysia is sustained through recurring narratives and institutional discourse, shaping tensions across multiple spheres of society.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt exploring National Harmony Commission, law to tackle racial tensions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is in the early stages of establishing a National Harmony Commission and drafting a Harmony Act, Deputy National Unity Minister Yuneswaran Ramaraj said.
"We are in the midst of discussions to have a National Harmony Commission. We really want to have it," he told reporters after the launch of Pusat Komas' Malaysia Racism Report 2025 at the Sultan Abdul Samad Building today.
Yuneswaran said legislation in the form of a Harmony Act was also being considered.
"The time has come for us to draft that. There is a need to consolidate legislation so we can advocate more effectively on matters of harmony and unity," he said, adding that feedback was still being gathered from the Prime Minister's Office and other stakeholders.
Pusat Komas director Jerald Joseph, whose organisation recommended the commission's establishment in the report, said it must be independent, with empowered commissioners and authority beyond making recommendations.
He said cases involving racial or religious elements should be brought before the commission, which would have powers to investigate, summon those involved, issue warnings and impose sanctions, including barring individuals from public speaking for 30 to 60 days.
"So the police are not the first stage," he said, describing a graduated response in which the commission would first seek to de-escalate before referring matters to law enforcement.
"They would have powers to investigate, sanction, reduce tensions and make recommendations to improve the system," he added.
The Malaysia Racism Report 2025 called for the urgent establishment of the commission as an independent body to address racial and religious conflicts, promote unity and social cohesion, advise the government and enforcement agencies, and provide training for civil servants.
Calls for such a commission have been made by various quarters, including
national unity advocate Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two houses, surau destroyed in Keningau fire
Article:
KENINGAU: Two houses and a surau were destroyed in a fire at Kampung Pagar-Pagar Sondoton here late last night.
Acting Keningau Fire and Rescue Station officer Evan Bobby said the team received an emergency call at 12.53am, before a 10-member crew was dispatched to the scene.
"Upon arrival at 1am, the fire had engulfed two houses and a surau completely," he said in a statement today.
He added that the operation concluded at 3am with no casualties reported, and that the cause of the fire is still under investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysians in Qatar advised to follow safety guidelines amid school reopening
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian citizens in Qatar are advised to adhere strictly to safety instructions and guidelines issued by Qatari authorities following the reopening of schools in the country.
In a statement, the Malaysian Embassy in Doha said that compliance was essential to ensure the safety of students and maintain a secure learning environment for all.
"With the reopening of schools, all Malaysians in Qatar are advised to consistently follow the instructions and safety guidelines set by Qatari authorities," it said in a statement posted on its Facebook page yesterday.
The embassy added that the situation in Qatar was currently calm and under control, with most daily activities having resumed as normal.
"However, all Malaysians in Qatar are advised to remain vigilant and alert to developments in their surroundings," it said.
Qatar's Ministry of Education and Higher Education announced last week the gradual resumption of in-person classes across the country's educational institutions from March 24, with full attendance for all students in schools and kindergartens resuming on March 29, according to Qatar News Agency (QNA).
Earlier, on Feb 28, the ministry announced that all kindergartens, public and private schools, educational centres, and universities would shift to distance learning from March 1 as a precautionary measure until further notice.
The embassy also reminded Malaysians in Qatar to regularly check the latest flight schedules when planning travel, as commercial flights remain subject to changes.
"Please comply with all instructions issued by the Qatari authorities and do not record or disseminate information relating to security incidents, as it is an offence under Qatari law," the statement added.
Malaysians requiring assistance or information regarding affected fellow citizens can contact the Embassy by phone at +974 3374 6733 / +974 4483 6463, or +603 8887 4570 (Wisma Putra Operations Room in Putrajaya), or via email at mwdoha@kln.gov.my.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia poised to attract tourists amid Middle East crisis
Article:
TAIPING: A strategic shift in tourism positioning will be key to ensuring the success of Visit Malaysia 2026, amid ongoing disruptions caused by the Middle East crisis.
State Tourism, Industry, Investment and Corridor Development Committee chairman Loh Sze Yee said while no country was immune to the spillover effects of the crisis, Malaysia was in a relatively favourable position due to its stability and ample energy supply.
"Of course, in tourism, no country in the world is spared from the effects of what is happening in the Middle East. However, we need to adjust our strategies to address the current situation," he told reporters after launching the refurbished interior of the Taiping Clock Tower today.
Loh said that several countries were grappling with rising fuel prices and supply shortages, which have disrupted travel and increased operational costs.
"Malaysia is fortunate. If we look at neighbouring nations, many have raised fuel prices. Not only have prices gone up, but some are also facing shortages. Here, fuel prices remain stable and supplies sufficient. This is a key factor in attracting tourists. Generally, tourists prefer stable destinations. They do not want to travel to places facing unrest or uncertainty. This stability is crucial in promoting Malaysia," he said.
He added that the Middle East played a critical role as a global aviation hub, and any instability in the region had far-reaching consequences for flight connectivity.
"When crises occur, flights are not only cancelled due to fuel constraints but also because of safety concerns. Airlines are forced to take longer routes, resulting in higher costs and longer travel times," said Loh.
He said Malaysia should pivot its tourism strategy towards Eastern markets and neighbouring countries, particularly targeting high-income travellers affected by instability in their home countries.
"For the business community, certain adjustments can be made. But for tourists, we believe there is a need to focus on the Eastern region, including neighbouring countries. Among higher-income groups in neighbouring nations experiencing instability, it may be beneficial for them to come to Malaysia temporarily. In their home countries, even if they have money, they may face difficulties accessing fuel. They can come to Malaysia to stay temporarily or even work here, especially with the rise of remote working," he said.
He said that Malaysia also stood to benefit from travellers unable to proceed with plans to Western destinations.
"As people often say, if you want to experience India, come to Malaysia; if you want to experience China, come to Malaysia; if you want to experience the Malay world, come to Malaysia. We have everything here. This puts us in a strong position to promote tourism, particularly given our stable energy supply, which strengthens our offerings," he added.
Loh said that the country's stable energy resources further bolstered its position in promoting tourism amid global uncertainty.
On domestic tourism, he said Perak remained one of the strongest contributors and could see increased activity as Malaysians opt to travel locally.
"This may not entirely be a crisis for us. In fact, it could benefit states like Perak as more Malaysians choose domestic destinations when overseas travel becomes less feasible," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Blogger Papagomo fined RM4,000 for seditious remarks against King
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Blogger Wan Muhammad Azri Wan Deris, also known as Papagomo, was fined RM4,000 for making seditious statements that incited hatred against His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, the King of Malaysia.
Sessions Court Judge Norma Ismail handed down the sentence after the accused failed to raise reasonable doubt against the prosecution's case today.
He was also ordered to serve four months in jail if he fails to pay the fine.
Earlier, deputy public prosecutor Sabri Othman sought a deterrent sentence, saying it should serve as a lesson for the public.
"The accused insulted the Yang di-Pertuan Agong using harsh words. A fine alone would not suffice, as this is a serious offence," he said.
Lawyer Muhammad Rafique Rashid Ali, representing Wan Muhammad Azri, requested a lenient sentence, citing that his client had five children to support, including an autistic child.
"He has cooperated fully in the case. This is his first offence involving sedition," he said.
On May 2, 2024, Wan Muhammad Azri pleaded not guilty to posting a seditious publication on his X account regarding Sultan Ibrahim's audience with the owner of KK Supermart & Superstore Sdn Bhd.
He was charged under Section 4(1)(c) of the Sedition Act 1948, which carries a maximum fine of RM5,000, or imprisonment for up to three years, or both, upon conviction.
He was ordered to enter his defence on Sept 24 last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Motorist rear-ended after stopping for police escort sparks debate online [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A viral video of a car being rear-ended after stopping at a traffic light for a police motorcycle escort has sparked debate among netizens over liability in such situations.
The short clip, widely circulated on social media, shows a vehicle coming to a stop at a junction, believed to be in compliance with instructions from a police outrider managing traffic.
Moments later, the stationary car is struck from behind by another vehicle, resulting in damage to its rear bumper.
In the footage, the police outrider only comes into view after the collision, dismounting his motorcycle before proceeding to direct traffic at the junction.
The damaged car is then seen reversing out of the way as the officer continues managing the flow of vehicles.
Towards the end of the clip, the policeman is seen walking towards the affected vehicle before the video cuts off.
The incident has triggered discussions online, with several users questioning who should be held responsible in such circumstances, particularly when motorists are required to stop abruptly for police escorts.
Some argued that drivers should always maintain a safe distance to prevent such collisions, while others pointed to the challenges of reacting to sudden stops at busy junctions.
The New Straits Times has reached out to the police for clarification, including standard procedures involving traffic control by police escorts and liability in the event of an accident.
The location of the incident has yet to be determined.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Racist remarks in Parliament 'deeply saddening', says Yuneswaran
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Deputy National Unity Minister Yuneswaran Ramaraj has expressed concern over racist remarks made by members of parliament, after a report identified Parliament as the largest source of recorded incidents last year.
Speaking at the launch of Pusat Komas' Malaysia Racism Report 2025 at the Sultan Abdul Samad Building today, he said he was "very sad" to see such remarks coming from fellow parliamentarians.
Yuneswaran said the matter would be raised with Dewan Rakyat Speaker Tan Sri Johari Abdul.
"We have immunity, but it comes with responsibility. Every word we say is watched and taken seriously," he said, questioning why senior MPs in particular were making such statements.
He noted that nearly 80 per cent of Parliament-related cases highlighted in the report involved opposition MPs, but stressed that the issue reflected on all lawmakers.
"Parliament is a respected institution — one the public is watching closely," he said, adding that clearer guidelines were needed to prevent similar incidents.
Yuneswaran also pointed out that remarks made in Parliament are recorded in the Hansard and preserved as official records.
"Having immunity does not mean we can say whatever we want. Every remark has consequences," he said, adding that he personally avoids statements that could provoke tension.
He said the ministry would prepare a report and seek a meeting with the Speaker to address concerns over royalty, race and religion (3R)-related remarks in the chamber. The matter would also be raised with National Unity Minister Datuk Aaron Ago Dagang.
"I take this report very seriously. It highlights this as the number one issue, and it is something we must address," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police search for mother of newborn abandoned in Ampang rubbish bin
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are still searching for the
mother
of a newborn baby girl found abandoned in a rubbish bin outside a house in Taman Putra, Ampang, last Wednesday.
Ampang Jaya police chief Assistant Commissioner Khairul Anuar Khalid, said the woman, believed to be the infant's mother, had yet to be located.
"The suspect, who is the baby's mother, is still being traced. Further developments will be announced later," he said in a brief statement today.
The newborn, believed to be about four hours old when discovered, was found in a bin outside a local resident's home at 6.27pm on March 25. She still had her umbilical cord attached.
The infant was rushed to Ampang Hospital, where she is in stable condition and undergoing treatment and monitoring.
The case is being investigated under Section 317 of the Penal Code for child abandonment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump: Iran sending US '20 big boats of oil'
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Sunday that Iran is sending the United States (US) 20 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz starting Monday morning, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
"And now, today, they gave us, as a tribute -- I don't know, I can't define it exactly, but they gave us, I think out of a sign of respect, 20 boats of oil -- big, big boats of oil -- going through the Hormuz Strait, and that's taking place starting tomorrow morning," he told reporters aboard Air Force One en route to Washington, DC.
"I would only say that we're doing extremely well in that negotiation," Trump said.
He added that he is "pretty sure" the US will make a deal with Iran, "but it's possible we won't."
"I do see a deal in Iran," he said, adding that it "could be soon."
Trump also said the US is negotiating with Iran both "directly and indirectly."
When asked whether he is still considering putting boots on the ground, he said the US has "lots of alternatives."
"We're ahead of schedule with Iran. We're weeks ahead of schedule...and we have a group, it's really a new regime.
It's a new group of people, people that we've never dealt with before, that are acting very reasonable," he said.
Iran last Wednesday rejected a 15-point plan from the US to end the war, saying any ceasefire would take place only on Tehran's terms and timeline.
When Trump was asked whether Iran had responded to his proposed 15-point plan, he said: "Yeah...They gave us most of the points -- why wouldn't they?"
"Just to prove that they're serious, they gave us all of these boats," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King briefed on Online Safety Act 2025
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, was briefed today on the Online Safety Act 2025 at Istana Bukit Tunku.
According to a post on His Majesty's official Facebook account, Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil presented details of the Act.
Fahmi said the Act also seeks to address the issue of fake accounts targeting the Malay Rulers and the Royal Family.
The Act aims to make the internet safer for families and children by establishing clear responsibilities for online platforms.
It includes an age verification mechanism to prevent children under 16 from creating social media accounts.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: G7 ministers set to tackle financial fallout of Mideast war
Article:
PARIS, France: Ministers from the G7 will hold talks on Monday to unpack the economic consequences of the war in the Middle East, the French government said, as oil and gas prices continue to soar.
The United States and Israel launched strikes on Iran in late February, and Tehran has hit back by targeting crude-exporting countries in the region and halting shipments through the Gulf.
The squeeze on supply has pushed oil and natural gas prices higher, with drastic knock-on effects for supply chains in countless industries.
French Finance Minister Roland Lescure said the G7 meeting, to be held via videoconference, would include energy and finance ministers as well as central bank chiefs and the heads of other international agencies.
"There are already differences in the responses largely linked to differences in exposure to the crisis," Lescure told a news conference on Friday, stressing that Asia was particularly exposed to the turmoil.
"That is one of the reasons why we wanted to convene a G7 of finance, energy and central banks," he said.
He added that the idea was to exchange views on the impact on financial markets and the economy, later telling local media it was the first time in half a century the G7 had used this format.
The G7, an informal grouping of the US, Canada, Britain, France, Germany, Italy and Japan, helps shape policy debates in the world's wealthiest nations.
The United States has sought support from the group to help halt Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz shipping route.
After a meeting last week, G7 foreign ministers said it was an "absolute necessity" for Iran to re-establish free passage through the strait and called for an end to attacks on civilian infrastructure.
GOVERNMENTS SCRAMBLING
Under increasing pressure, many governments have rolled out measures to limit the impact of supply difficulties and soaring energy prices.
But a lack of clarity over US war aims, along with uncertainty over the potential length of the conflict and the spread of hostilities, has left governments scrambling for coherent responses.
US officials, including President Donald Trump, have said their goals in the war are almost achieved, but thousands of US personnel have been sent to the region in an unprecedented military build-up.
Activists based outside Iran say the US-Israeli campaign has killed more than 3,000 people in the country, over half of them civilians, while Lebanese officials have said more than 1,000 have been killed there since Israel began attacking its territory in retaliation for Hezbollah attacks on Mar 2.
Officials in Israel and countries across the Gulf have also reported much smaller numbers of casualties.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Motorcyclist's bid to outrun traffic police ends in swift arrest
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A video showing a young motorcyclist's failed attempt to evade traffic police has gone viral, drawing both criticism and amusement from netizens.
The 60-second clip captures the moment a rider in a green T-shirt is flagged down by a traffic officer at a busy junction.
In the opening seconds, the officer is seen gesturing for the rider to pull over. Although he appears to comply, the rider suddenly revs his engine and speeds off as the officer turns to manage traffic.
The attempted escape is short-lived.
Mounted on a high-powered patrol motorcycle, the officer immediately gives chase. Dashcam footage from the police bike shows a brief pursuit through a suburban area lined with palm trees.
Despite attempts to weave through traffic, the rider is caught in under a minute and forced to stop by the roadside.
Visibly shaken, he appears to plead his case as he is confronted by the officer.
Netizens were quick to react, with many praising the officer's swift response.
"He thought he was in a movie. Bro, you can't outrun a police bike that easily," one user commented.
Another wrote: "The face of regret at the end says it all. Just follow the rules — it's cheaper than the fine for running away."
The exact location and date of the incident have not been confirmed.
Under Section 186 of the Penal Code, obstructing a public servant in the discharge of their duties carries a penalty of up to two years' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM10,000, or both.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia to halve tax on fuel, underwrite spot cargoes
Article:
PERTH/SYDNEY: Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Monday the government would halve the excise on fuel and diesel and remove the heavy road user charge for three months to help households cope with a surge in costs driven by the Iran war.
Halving the tax would reduce the cost of fuel by 26.3 Australian cents per litre, Albanese said.
The total cost to the government would be about A$2.55 billion (US$1.75 billion), Treasurer Jim Chalmers said at a joint press conference with the prime minister and Energy Minister Chris Bowen.
Some 20 per cent of the world's oil was transported through the Strait of Hormuz before the US-Israel war against Iran choked off supplies. The price of Brent has surged 59 per cent in March, the steepest monthly gain in history, and was at US$115.66 per barrel when the market opened on Monday.
The measures are the latest in a series of fuel security measures adopted by Canberra since the war began on Feb 28. It has previously announced the release of petrol and diesel from domestic reserves and the temporary relaxation of fuel quality standards.
The average retail price of a litre of diesel rose to more than A$3 in Australia last week and petrol to A$2.50, according to a March 29 report from the Australian Petroleum Institute.
"We understand the cost pressures for people are very real as the impact of the war on the other side of the world plays out right here," Albanese told the press conference in Canberra.
KEEPING THE COUNTRY MOVING
The government also confirmed it had adopted a national fuel security plan with all state governments agreeing to work together to deliver fuel to regional areas where it is needed most.
Albanese said there were four levels of action and Australia was at level two, which was to keep the country moving. The highest level would be to ensure critical services were maintained.
"While Australia's fuel supply outlook remains secure in the near term, we need to be very clear as well with Australians that the longer this war goes on, the worse the impacts will be," Albanese said.
Australia has its highest fuel stocks in 15 years, but they are still far below International Energy Agency recommendations of 90 days.
The latest government numbers as of last week said Australia had enough for 30 days of diesel, 30 days of jet fuel and 39 days of petrol.
It has also adopted the Export Finance and Insurance Corporation Amendment which will allow the government to underwrite the purchase of spot cargoes of fuel as prices rise via credit agency Export Finance Australia.
Bowen said that spot cargoes were available but expensive and smaller industry players were not able to take the risk.
"These new fuel security powers will put Australia in a strong position, securing fuel supply where it may be cost prohibitive for private suppliers to source on commercial terms, without government support," Bowen said in a statement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: In Seremban, babysitter jailed one year, fined RM15,000 over death of ‘Baby Syifaa’ four years ago
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — A babysitter was sentenced to one year’s imprisonment by the Sessions Court in Seremban today for negligence that led to the death of 15-month-old “Baby Syifaa” four years ago.
Judge N Kanageswari handed down the sentence to Khairunnisa Ahmad Damanhuri, 41, at the conclusion of the trial involving the incident at a childcare centre in Bandar Sri Sendayan, according to a report published in
Berita Harian
today.
The court also imposed a RM15,000 fine, with an additional four months’ jail in default of payment.
Khairunnisa was further ordered to complete 48 hours of community service within six months after serving her jail term, and to be placed under a one-year good behaviour bond of RM5,000 with one surety.
She was represented by lawyer S Kavittha, while the prosecution was led by Negeri Sembilan prosecution director Ku Hayati Ku Haron.
Also present in court were Baby Syifaa’s parents, Anida Idrus, 44, and Yuseri Yusoff, 42.
According to the charge, Khairunnisa, who had custody of the child, Nour Rania Asyifaa, had neglected the toddler in a manner likely to cause physical injury.
The offence was alleged to have taken place at about 12.30pm on February 22, 2022 at a childcare centre in Bandar Sri Sendayan.
She was charged under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001, which provides for up to 20 years’ imprisonment, a fine of up to RM50,000, or both, upon conviction.
On July 31, 2024, the Coroner’s Court ruled that the child’s death, after she was found unconscious in a cradle at the centre, was caused by pressure to the neck.
A total of 28 witnesses, including the child’s parents, testified during the inquest proceedings, which began on September 22, 2022.

Title: Penang says water supply secure for two months amid dry spell
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, March 30 — Penang’s water supply is expected to remain stable over the next two months despite the prolonged hot and dry weather.
Penang Water Supply Corporation (PBAPP) chief executive officer Datuk K. Pathmanathan said the current reserves in the Muda Dam and Beris Dam in Kedah are sufficient to support water supply through April and May.
He said the Muda Dam capacity has dropped to a critical 7.19 per cent as of March 28 and can no longer release water into Sungai Muda after significant drawdowns earlier this year for irrigation.
The capacity of the 122,400 ML Beris Dam was at 100 per cent on January 1 and its capacity has dropped to 58.06 per cent as of March 28.
“In the period from February 20 to March 28, its capacity was reduced from 78.1 per cent to 58.06 per cent in 36 days,” he said in a statement issued today.
He said this reflects an average dip of 0.56 per cent per day (685MLD).
“At this dipping level of 685 MLD, the remaining reserves of the Beris Dam (71,065ML) should be able to last for about 68 days until the 20 per cent critical level is reached in mid-or late June,” he said.
The capacity of the 122,400 ML Beris Dam was at 100 per cent on January 1 and its capacity has dropped to 58.06 per cent as of March 28. — Picture courtesy of PBAPP
As such, he said Penang is not under immediate threat, but this also depends on avoiding further heavy drawdowns that could deplete Beris Dam prematurely, he added.
Sungai Muda is Penang’s primary raw water source, from which PBAPP abstracts about 1,000 million litres per day (MLD), supplying water to some 465,000 consumers statewide.
Following the drop in Muda Dam levels, PBAPP has activated its Sungai Muda Contingency Plan 2026, including maximising river abstraction, drawing from the Expanded Mengkuang Dam, and maintaining water transfers to Penang Island.
Pathmanathan said these measures are helping to stabilise supply but they are not a complete solution as the Sungai Dua water treatment plant requires about 1,000 MLD of raw water daily.
He said PBAPP is carefully managing the effective capacities of Penang’s three key dams to avoid water shortages.
As of March 28, the effective capacities of Air Itam Dam is at 69.38 per cent, Teluk Bahang Dam at 84.05 per cent, and the Expanded Mengkuang Dam (EMD) at 91.24 per cent.
He called on consumers to use water wisely until the next rainy season.
“Due to climate change, we are uncertain when the next rainy season will arrive to “recharge” the reserves of the key dams in Penang and Kedah to safe levels,” he said.
He said domestic water consumption accounted for about 55.7 per cent (482 MLD) of the total consumption of water in Penang (865 MLD) last year.

Title: Farmers’ organisation board staff pleads not guilty to RM141,872 embezzlement in Johor Bahru
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, March 30 — A 36-year-old staff of the Farmers’ Organisation Board (LPP) claimed trial in the Sessions Court here today to 16 charges of fraudulently misappropriating property belonging to the Kota Tinggi Timur Farmers’ Organisation totalling RM141,872.67 over the past five years.
Muhammad Faiz Ibrahim pleaded not guilty after the charges were read out before Judge Datuk Ahmad Kamal Arifin Ismail.
Based on the charge sheets, the accused, who is also the Kota Tinggi Timur Area Farmers’ Organisation’s economic affairs assistant officer and manager, is charged with fraudulently embezzling proceeds from the sale of palm oil Fresh Fruit Bunches (FFB) totalling RM141,872.67 that belonged to the organisation.
The act was allegedly committed at the office of the Kota Tinggi Timur Farmers’ Development Centre in Mawai Baru, Kota Tinggi, between April 26, 2021 and November 15, 2022.
The accused was charged under Section 403 of the Penal Code for fraudulent misappropriation of property which carries a maximum prison sentence of five years, whipping and also a fine upon conviction.
During the proceedings, the accused also claimed trial to 16 optional charges of criminal breach of trust by using the property entrusted to him, namely the palm oil FFB, totalling RM141,872.67 for his personal use by selling the fruit and depositing the proceeds of the sale into his account.
For the act committed at the same location and time, the accused was charged under Section 406 of the Penal Code for criminal breach of trust which provides for a maximum prison sentence of 10 years, whipping and also a fine upon conviction.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), prosecuting officer Alice Voon Mei Ling offered bail of RM25,000, but was objected by the accused’s counsel Asiah Abd Jalil.
According to Asiah, her client, who is single, has been serving the LPP since 2011 and draws an income of RM4,000 per month.
“Despite not being married, the accused has to support both his parents who are former Felda settlers.
“My client, who resides with his family in Kulai, has been cooperative since the MACC investigation started and he is not at risk of fleeing,” she said.
The court then allowed the accused bail amount of RM10,000 with a single surety and set April 30 for mention and the submission of documents.

Title: Three week water disruption in Gua Musang’s Rantau Manis forces residents to bathe at R&R and rivers
Article:
GUA MUSANG, March 30 — Some residents of Taman Agropolitan Rantau Manis here have been forced to bathe at highway rest and service areas (R&R), rivers, and transport water from the town located about 40 kilometres away due to the water supply disruption that has persisted for three weeks.
Sahazudin Mohammad, 52, said he had to rely on R&R facilities to bathe despite living in his own home.
“This water supply disruption has made daily life difficult for the residents, especially during Ramadan and the Aidilfitri celebrations.
“Water supply is only available from around midnight until 6am, and even then, the flow is very slow. Air Kelantan Sdn Bhd (AKSB) has been refilling water tanks, but it is still insufficient for residents in this area,” he said when met by reporters here today.
Meanwhile, Mohd Nasah Ya, 55, said some families have had to spend hundreds of ringgit to purchase clean drinking water.
“I also have to travel back and forth from my home in Rantau Manis to my family’s house in Gua Musang town, about 40km away, just to obtain water,” he said.
According to the AKSB Corporate Department, the supply disruption was caused by the water treatment plant (LRA) exceeding its maximum capacity due to a sharp increase in demand, particularly during the festive season.
The Limau Kasturi treatment plant, which supplies water to Rantau Manis, is currently operating at 5.2 million litres per day, which is above its actual capacity of 4 million litres.
To mitigate the situation, AKSB is providing water supply assistance by deploying tanker lorries, refilling static tanks in affected areas twice daily for now. — Bernama

Title: Selangor exco calls for maximum jail and caning for fatal drink-driving cases
Article:
SHAH ALAM, March 30 — A Selangor state executive councillor has called for tougher penalties, including maximum imprisonment and caning, for drink-driving offences following a fatal crash in Klang yesterday.
State Human Resources and Poverty Eradication Committee chairman V. Papparaidu said existing measures appeared insufficient as a deterrent, as evidenced by the rising number of drink-driving cases.
“I urge that firm and commensurate legal action be taken against the perpetrator… this is no longer an isolated case, as far too many such tragedies have occurred.
“It is time for a tougher approach through stricter enforcement and heavier punishment to serve as a lesson to the public. Appropriate assistance should also be promptly extended to the victim’s family, especially when the deceased was the head of the household, in the interest of upholding justice and preventing such tragedies from recurring.
“The lives of the people should not be put at risk due to the irresponsible actions of a few. Roads must be safe for all…enough is enough! Firm action must be prioritised for everyone’s safety!” he said in a Facebook post.
Yesterday, Klang Selatan district police chief ACP Lim Jit Huey said a motorcyclist was killed after being hit by a car driven by an individual under the influence of alcohol and drugs on Jalan Raya Barat.
He said the incident, which occurred at about 11.40 am, resulted in the victim, a man in his 30s, being flung onto the roof of another car. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
This morning, the Klang Magistrate’s Court ordered the suspect, in his 20s, to be remanded for four days until Thursday to assist investigations under Section 44(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 and Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
Papparaidu also extended his condolences to the victim’s family and prayed they would be given strength and resilience in facing the ordeal.
“I express my deepest sorrow and sympathy over the tragic accident on Jalan Raya Barat, Klang, which claimed the life of an innocent motorcyclist, who was also a father of two young children.
“This incident once again proves that driving under the influence of alcohol and drugs is extremely dangerous. It is even more heartbreaking when such negligence claims the life of a completely innocent individual,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Ex-Army chief Hafizuddeain and wife to face joint trial over illegal proceeds
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — The 11-count case of receiving money from illegal activities involving former Army Chief (PTD) Tan Sri Muhammad Hafizuddeain Jantan and his wife, Salwani Anuar @ Kamaruddin will be tried together at the Sessions Court here.
Judge Azura Alwi decided this after allowing the application of deputy public prosecutor Mahadi Abdul Jumaat for the cases to be tried together and it was not objected by lawyer Aizul Rohan Anuar who represented Muhammad Hafizuddeain and lawyer Fahmi Abd Moin representing Salwani.
“The court allowed the prosecution’s application for the cases to be tried together and set May 18 for mention of the case for the submission of documents. The bail of both accused has previously been maintained,” said Azura during the mention of the case.
Earlier, Mahadi said Muhammad Hafizuddeain was previously charged at the Shah Alam Sessions Court and the case was transferred to the Sessions Court here, while Salwani was charged at the Kuala Terengganu Sessions Court and the Kuala Lumpur Sessions Court.
“The prosecution requested that all these cases be tried together in accordance with the provisions of Section 91 of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 (AMLATFPUAA), Section 165 and Section 170 of the Criminal Procedure Code (CPC).
“The reason for this application is because it involves the same witnesses and facts of the case as well as the same predicate offence. Therefore, please request that these cases be tried together and request that a case mention date be set for the submission of documents,” he said.
On January 22, Muhammad Hafizuddeain Jantan was charged at the Sessions Court here on four counts of receiving proceeds of illegal activities, namely money amounting to RM969,000, RM474,850, RM488,550 and RM190,000 which were deposited into three bank accounts belonging to him.
All the offences were allegedly committed at three banks, involving three different locations, namely at Jalan Melaka, Bukit Damansara and the city centre here between February 2 2024 and November 7 2025, under Subsection 4(1)(b) AMLATFPUAA and punishable under Subsection 4(1) of the same act.
The section provides for a maximum imprisonment of 15 years and a fine of not less than five times the amount or proceeds of the illegal activity or RM5 million, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
On the same date, his wife Salwani Anuar was charged with four counts of receiving proceeds from illegal activities, amounting to RM50,000, RM7,000, RM10,000 and RM10,000 respectively, which were deposited into a bank account belonging to her at a branch of the bank at Damansara Heights, Wisma UOA II here, between November 24 2024 and November 25 2025.
Charges under Subsection 4(1)(b) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 and read together with Subsection 87(1)(a) of the same act, and punishable under Subsection 4(1) of the same act which carries the same punishment.
On January 23, Muhammad Hafizuddeain was charged at the Shah Alam Sessions Court on two counts of receiving proceeds of illegal activities, namely money amounting to RM115,000 and RM30,000 which were deposited into a bank account belonging to him at a bank branch, at Ampang Point, Ampang between February 2, 2024 to May 19, 2025 and he was charged under Subsection 4(1)(b) of the AMLATFPUAA which carries the same punishment.
Salwani was also charged at the Kuala Terengganu Sessions Court on January 26 on a charge of receiving money amounting to RM5,000 which was the proceeds of illegal activities, which was deposited into her bank account
The offence was allegedly committed at a bank branch in Kerteh near Besut, on January 16 2025 under Subsection 4(1) (b) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 (Act 613) and punishable under Subsection 4(1) of the same act.
If convicted, the accused can be sentenced to imprisonment for up to 15 years and a fine of not less than five times the amount or value of the proceeds of the illegal activity or instrumentalities of the offence at the time the offence was committed, or RM5 million, whichever is higher. — Bernama

Title: Malaysians in Qatar urged to follow safety rules as schools reopen
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 30 — Malaysian citizens in Qatar are advised to always comply with the safety instructions and guidelines issued by Qatari authorities following the reopening of schools in the country.
The Embassy of Malaysia in Doha, Qatar, said in a release that compliance is important to ensure the safety of students and a secure learning environment for all.
“With the reopening of schools, all Malaysians in Qatar are advised to constantly comply with the instructions and safety guidelines set by the Qatari authorities,” according to a statement uploaded on the Embassy’s Facebook page on Sunday.
The Embassy stated that the situation in Qatar is currently calm and under control, with most daily activities having resumed as normal.
“However, all Malaysians in Qatar are advised to remain vigilant and stay alert to developments in their surroundings,” it added.
